title: Accountability for Veterans Act
plain_summary: The Accountability for Veterans Act requires the Department of Veterans Affairs to write a report about problems affecting veterans. The report must explain why disability benefit appeals are taking too long and getting backed up. It also needs to describe how the VA can better help soldiers when they leave the military and return to civilian life. The report must also look at management problems in the VA health care systems that have the lowest ratings. The VA has six months to finish this report and send it to Congress.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Veterans' Affairs.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Accountability for Veterans Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Report on Certain Appeals, Resources, and Health Care Systems of the Department of Veterans Affairs
sections.text: Within one hundred and eighty days after this law becomes official, the head of the Department of Veterans Affairs must send a report to the Veterans Affairs committees in both the House of Representatives and the Senate. This report must explain three things. First, it must explain why there is a backlog of appeals for disability benefits claims that the Department handles. A backlog means there are more appeals waiting to be processed than the Department can handle quickly. Second, the report must suggest ways to give more information, resources, and tools to military service members and their spouses who are in the Transition Assistance Program. This program helps people leaving the military adjust to civilian life and is described in sections eleven forty-two and eleven forty-four of title ten of the United States Code. Third, the report must describe the ongoing management problems that affect one-star health care systems within the Department of Veterans Affairs. One-star systems are those that have received the lowest quality rating.
